title: From Hardware Gates to "Thinking" Machines
concept: Early "intelligent" machines were built entirely from hard-wired logic gates that engineers designed by hand — which raised a new question: could a machine ever learn its own rules instead?

exercises.prompt: Recap: Wiring two switches in parallel so that either one can turn on a light behaves like which gate?
exercises.explain: Exactly! You've now built gates out of real circuits. Time for a bit of history.

cards.title: Rooms full of clicking switches
cards.body: Long before laptops, some of the earliest calculating machines were rooms full of mechanical relays — physically clicking open and closed thousands of times a second, each one just another AND, OR, or NOT gate.

cards.title: Boolean logic made it official
cards.body: In 1854, mathematician George Boole showed that true/false reasoning could be written as algebra — what we now call Boolean logic. In 1937, engineer Claude Shannon proved that electrical switching circuits could implement that exact algebra, gate for gate.

cards.title: A recipe written by hand
cards.body: Building one of these early machines was like writing a recipe with zero shortcuts: an engineer had to decide every single gate, every single wire, for every single rule the machine would ever follow.

cards.title: Hard-wired, not learned
cards.body: Want the machine to check a new rule? An engineer had to physically redesign the circuit — rewiring gates by hand. Nothing about these early machines learned anything from experience; every rule was planned in advance, by a person.

cards.title: "Electronic" didn't mean "smart"
cards.body: It's easy to assume any machine built from wires and switches was already some form of artificial intelligence. It wasn't — it was just very fast, very literal logic. It could follow rules perfectly, but it could not discover a rule on its own.

left.text:
A human designs every gate
by hand, rule by rule

right.text:
Could a machine ever learn
its own gate-like rules from examples instead?

exercises.prompt: What did George Boole formalize in 1854 that later became the basis for logic gates?

options.text: An algebra for reasoning with true and false
A machine that learns from data

exercises.explain: Right! Boole's algebra gave true/false reasoning exact mathematical rules — the foundation Shannon later built circuits on.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Early hard-wired logic machines could learn new rules automatically from examples.
exercises.explain: False. Every rule had to be designed and wired in by a human engineer — nothing was learned automatically.

exercises.sentence: In 1937, Claude Shannon showed that electrical switching circuits could implement ___ logic.
exercises.explain: Shannon connected Boole's true/false algebra directly to real electrical switching circuits.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes the limitation of early hard-wired logic machines?

options.text: Every rule had to be designed and wired in by a human engineer, one gate at a time
They automatically improved their own rules the more they were used
They had no logic gates at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Hard-wired machines were powerful but rigid — a human had to plan every rule in advance.

exercises.prompt: An engineer in the 1940s wants their machine to handle a brand-new rule it wasn't originally built for. Based on how hard-wired logic gates worked, what must happen?

options.text: The engineer must physically redesign and rewire the circuit by hand — the machine can't learn the rule on its own
The machine studies some examples overnight and updates its own wiring
Nothing — the machine already knows every possible rule
The rule is impossible to represent with logic gates

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Hard-wiring every rule by hand doesn't scale — and that limitation is exactly what pushed researchers to ask a bold new question: what if a machine could learn its own gate-like rules from examples, instead of being told them? That question is where our next chapter begins.
